title: SEGMENT 2 — Why pigs: anatomy, breeding speed, and the ethics line

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — Why pigs: anatomy, breeding speed, and the ethics line
So why a pig?
Why not, say, a chimpanzee, which is much more closely related to us — only one percent different in DNA?
Why not a sheep, which we already farm?
Why a pig?
There are three answers, and they all matter.
Anatomy.
Reproduction.
Ethics.
Let's take anatomy first.
A grown adult pig — a particular breed, a smaller breed I'll come back to — has organs that are remarkably similar in size and shape to adult human organs.
A pig heart is roughly the same size as a human heart.
A pig kidney is roughly the same size as a human kidney.
The vessels come off in roughly the right configuration.
The pressures the heart pumps at are roughly the right range.
If you look at a pig heart and a human heart side by side on a table — and surgeons have done this many, many times — you will see something startling.
The two are within, call it, ten or fifteen percent of each other on most dimensions.
A pig's heart will fit in the cavity left behind when you remove a human heart.
The pulmonary artery and the aorta line up.
The surgical anatomy is, broadly, compatible.
Compare this to a baboon.
A baboon weighs maybe thirty kilos.
Its heart is fine for a thirty-kilo animal, but if you transplant it into an eighty-kilo human, it cannot keep up with the metabolic demand.
The Baby Fae case in 1984 used a baboon heart in an infant precisely because an infant was small enough that the baboon heart could plausibly cope.
For an adult, you need an adult-sized organ from an animal of human-comparable mass.
Pigs fit.
Most other livestock — sheep, goats — are too small or their organs are the wrong shape.
Cattle are the right mass but their organs are quite different in geometry.
Pigs are, anatomically, the closest match we have outside of the great apes.
Now, reproduction.
A pig reaches sexual maturity in about six months.
A sow's gestation is three months, three weeks, three days — pig farmers will quote that to you.
A litter is typically eight to twelve piglets.
A pig will give birth twice a year.
So if you start with one breeding pair of genetically engineered pigs, in three years you can have a herd of thousands.
Compare this to chimpanzees, which take eight to ten years to reach reproductive maturity and have one offspring at a time, every five years or so.
There is no plausible commercial path to a chimpanzee organ supply.
The numbers just don't work.
With pigs, you can run a herd of the size you'd need to supply the United States.
You can run it on a single piece of farmland.
You can run it within a single business cycle.
And then, the ethics question.
This is the one most people don't think about until I bring it up, but it is, I think, the most important of the three.
We already kill, in the United States alone, about a hundred and thirty million pigs a year.
For bacon, for pork chops, for ham, for hot dogs, for the gelatin in your gummy bears.
As a society, we have already made a moral decision that the death of a pig is acceptable when the benefit is human nourishment — or, frankly, just human pleasure at lunchtime.
If we accept that for a sandwich, it is hard to argue that we shouldn't accept it to save a human life.
The moral cost of taking a pig's heart, when you're already taking ten thousand pig hearts a year for sausage, looks much lower than the moral cost of taking a chimpanzee's heart.
Chimpanzees and other great apes occupy a different ethical category.
They're our nearest cousins.
They show evidence of culture and tool use and mourning and self-recognition.
The entire community of bioethicists and primatologists agrees that taking organs from great apes is not on the table.
There were experiments with chimpanzee kidneys in the 1960s — they're done.
They're not coming back.
The line society has drawn is: not great apes, not for transplant.
Pigs sit on the other side of that line.
There's also the disease angle, which I'll spend a whole segment on later.
We share many viruses with chimpanzees, including HIV.
Cross-species transplant from a chimp carries a real risk of moving novel pathogens into humans — and into humans whose immune systems are pharmacologically suppressed, no less.
Pigs have their own viruses, but the genetic distance means the overlap with human pathogens is smaller.
That's also a vote for the pig.
Now, having said all of that, I want to be honest about a complication.
The pigs we use for xenotransplantation are not the pigs on a farm.

They are specifically bred and engineered — usually a breed called a Yucatan miniature pig or one of the Göttingen minipig lines, which max out at around the size of an adult human rather than continuing to grow to four hundred kilos like a commercial Yorkshire.
They're raised in extreme isolation, in facilities that look more like semiconductor fabs than barns, to keep them free of every infectious agent we can possibly exclude.
They live their entire short lives in a clean room.
Whether that's a better or worse life than a factory-farm pig is a question for a different lecture, but it's worth flagging that the xenotransplant pig is its own particular thing.
So: pigs, because the anatomy fits, the reproduction is fast, and the ethics line has been drawn between us and primates rather than us and livestock.
Cleared up.
Let's go talk about the wall that nearly killed the field.
